claim: Mixed-breed lost dogs traveled an average of 14 miles before being picked up, versus an average of only 2 miles for purebred dogs — a 7x differential attributable to perceived value signaling to potential rescuers.
stance_target: breed appearance as primary determinant of rescuer intervention distance
scope_conditions: 254 dog owners who lost and then found their dogs, surveyed 1999, USA
quoted_support: Mixed-breed dogs were allowed to travel an average of 14 miles before they were picked up, whereas purebred dogs were allowed to travel only an average of 2 miles before they were picked up.

claim: Toy breeds were the least likely to travel more than 0.75 miles from where they were last seen.
stance_target: small dog size as predictor of short search radius
scope_conditions: 1999 informal study, 254 recovered lost dogs, USA
quoted_support: Toy breeds were the least likely to travel more than three-quarters of a mile from where they were last seen.

claim: Pointing breeds traveled farther than retrieving breeds when lost, suggesting breed-specific drive types influence dispersal distance.
stance_target: breed behavioral type as dispersal distance modifier
scope_conditions: 1999 informal study of lost and recovered dogs
quoted_support: Breed and size seemed to have an effect on the distance traveled—pointing breeds were more likely to travel farther than retrieving breeds.

claim: Geographic barriers such as canyons, motorway walls, and rivers directly constrain and redirect a lost dog's direction of travel.
stance_target: terrain corridor modeling — hard barriers as directional constraints
scope_conditions: Lost dogs in varied terrain including urban fringe and natural environments
quoted_support: A canyon, a brick wall along a freeway, and a raging river are all geographical barriers that would inhibit and influence a dog's direction of flight.

claim: Fearful, skittish dogs that escape near isolated areas (wooded areas, golf courses, cemeteries, large parks) prefer to establish residence in those quiet areas rather than moving toward human activity.
stance_target: quiet isolated terrain as preferred habitat for fearful lost dogs
scope_conditions: Fearful-temperament dogs lost near low-traffic terrain features
quoted_support: We've seen that many dogs with fearful temperaments that escape near isolated areas (wooded areas, golf courses, cemeteries, large parks, etc.) prefer to remain in those quiet areas, but we do not know what percentage.
region: general (directly applicable to Algarve cork oak forests, nature parks)

claim: Lord et al. (2007) found 71% of 187 lost dogs were recovered; the remaining 29% fate is unknown, suggesting current recovery methods fail for roughly 1 in 3 lost dogs.
stance_target: current recovery rate baseline and gap
scope_conditions: 187 lost dogs in USA, data collected 2005, published 2007
quoted_support: The study determined that of the 187 dogs that were lost, 132 (71%) of them were recovered.
entities: Journal of the American Veterinary Medical Association

claim: Dogs escaping in low-population-density settings (isolated highway rest stops, rural Nevada) require a 30-mile poster radius at major highway exits, versus a very tight local area for high-density urban escapes.
stance_target: population density as primary determinant of search radius in sparse environments
scope_conditions: Lost dogs at highway transport escape points vs. urban centers
quoted_support: For the dog at the rest stop I would create giant, neon posters for all major exits from that highway within a 30-mile radius.
region: USA (applicable to sparse Algarve rural roads)
